symbols: flock of sheep — Jacob’s labor and servitude under Laban
well — the meeting place and source of life in the patriarchal narratives
woman with water jar — Rachel, whom Jacob met at the well
shepherd’s staff — the pastoral vocation and God’s providential care
vast open landscape — the land of sojourn, Haran, far from the Promised Land
description: A black and white engraving showing a pastoral scene. In the foreground, a young woman in robes stands near a stone well, carrying a large water jar on her shoulder. Behind her, a seated figure in robes and head covering tends a large flock of sheep spread across an open, hilly landscape. The sky is dramatic with clouds and atmospheric effects. The scene is rendered in fine cross-hatching typical of detailed engraving.
theological_description: Jacob’s years keeping Laban’s flock represent one of the most significant periods of patriarchal formation in Scripture. Genesis 29-31 records how Jacob served Laban fourteen years for his two wives Leah and Rachel, and additional years for flocks. It was at the well that Jacob first met Rachel and rolled away the stone to water her father’s sheep (Genesis 29:10)—an act that prefigures Christ as the Good Shepherd who provides living water. Jacob’s declaration to Laban reveals the cost of faithful shepherding: ‘These twenty years I have been in your house… the heat consumed me by day and the frost by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes’ (Genesis 31:38-40). This suffering servitude shaped Jacob into Israel, the father of the twelve tribes. The well scene also connects to the broader biblical pattern of betrothal at wells—Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 24), Moses and Zipporah (Exodus 2:15-21)—establishing the well as a place where God’s providence directs the course of salvation history.

scripture_refs.reason: Jacob’s arrival at the well, meeting Rachel, and beginning to tend Laban’s flock
Jacob’s shrewd management of the flocks and his growing prosperity under Laban
Jacob’s testimony of twenty years of faithful, costly shepherding
The Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep, prefigured in Jacob’s sacrificial care
